`yid` – inclusive code Individual codes: `ydd` – Eastern Yiddish `yih` – Western Yiddish

Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Hebrew "Help:Hebrew"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of Hebrew letters.

or **Jewish German**,[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[b]](#cite_note-15) is a [West Germanic language](/wiki/West_Germanic_language "West Germanic language") historically spoken by [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"). It originated in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and provided the nascent Ashkenazi community with a [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") based on [High German](/wiki/High_German "High German") fused with many elements taken from [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") (notably [Mishnaic](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew")) and to some extent [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"). Most varieties of Yiddish include elements of [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), and the vocabulary contains traces of [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages").[[14]](#cite_note-baumgarten-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-Yardumian-18) Yiddish has traditionally been written using the [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet").

**This article contains [Hebrew text](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Hebrew "Help:Hebrew"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of Hebrew letters.

**Yiddish**,[[a]](#cite_note-13) historically **Judeo-German** or **Jewish German**,[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[b]](#cite_note-15) is a [West Germanic language](/wiki/West_Germanic_language "West Germanic language") historically spoken by [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews"). It originated in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and provided the nascent Ashkenazi community with a [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") based on [High German](/wiki/High_German "High German") fused with many elements taken from [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") (notably [Mishnaic](/wiki/Mishnaic_Hebrew "Mishnaic Hebrew")) and to some extent [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"). Most varieties of Yiddish include elements of [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), and the vocabulary contains traces of [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages").[[14]](#cite_note-baumgarten-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-Yardumian-18) Yiddish has traditionally been written using the [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet").

For centuries, Yiddish was the vernacular of Ashkenazi communities. Starting from the late 18th century, under the impulse of the *[Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")* (Jewish Enlightenment), the language suffered decreasing [prestige](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)"), being stigmatized by [assimilationists](/wiki/Jewish_assimilation "Jewish assimilation") and later also [Zionists](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism"), in favor of national languages and Hebrew. Efforts by the [Yiddishist movement](/wiki/Yiddishist_movement "Yiddishist movement") to counter this met limited success. Nevertheless, before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the language still counted 11–13 million speakers.[[17]](#cite_note-yivoyiddish-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20) 85% of the approximately six million Jews who were murdered in the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") were Yiddish speakers,[[19]](#cite_note-Sprache_1984_p._3-21) leading to a massive decline in the use of the language. This, along with further assimilation following World War II and *[aliyah](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah")* (immigration to Israel), sped up the process of [language shift](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift") to national tongues and Modern Hebrew, particularly among secular Jews. The number of Yiddish speakers is, however, on the rise in [*Haredi*](/wiki/Haredi_Judaism "Haredi Judaism") (ultra-Orthodox) communities. In 2014, [YIVO](/wiki/YIVO "YIVO") stated that "most people who speak Yiddish in their daily lives are [*Hasidim*](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism") and other *Haredim*", whose population was estimated at the time to be between 500,000 and 1 million.[[20]](#cite_note-:5-22) A 2021 estimate from [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") was that there were 250,000 American speakers, 250,000 Israeli speakers, and 100,000 in the rest of the world (for a total of 600,000).[[2]](#cite_note-2021_stats-2)

The earliest surviving references date from the 12th century and call the language לשון־אַשכּנז‎ (*loshn-ashknaz*; lit. 'language of Ashkenaz') or טײַטש‎ (*taytsh*), a variant of *tiutsch*, the contemporary name for [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German"). Colloquially, the language is sometimes called מאַמע־לשון‎ (*mame-loshn*; lit. 'mother tongue'), distinguishing it from לשון־קודש‎ (*[loshn koydesh](/wiki/Lashon_Hakodesh "Lashon Hakodesh")*; lit. 'holy tongue'), meaning 'Hebrew and Aramaic'.[[c]](#cite_note-23) The term "Yiddish", short for *"Yidish-Taitsh"* ('Jewish German'), did not become the most frequently used designation in the literature until the 18th century. In the late 19th and into the 20th century, the language was more commonly called "Jewish", especially in non-Jewish contexts, but "Yiddish" is again the most common designation today.[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[20]](#cite_note-:5-22)

Modern Yiddish has [two major dialect groups](/wiki/Yiddish_dialects "Yiddish dialects"): Eastern and Western. Eastern Yiddish is far more common today. It includes Southeastern (Ukrainian–Romanian), Mideastern (Polish–Galician–Eastern Hungarian), and Northeastern (Lithuanian–Belarusian) dialects. Eastern Yiddish differs from Western Yiddish both by its far greater size and the extensive inclusion of words of Slavic origin. Western Yiddish, which is divided into Southwestern (Swiss–Alsatian–Southern German), Midwestern (Central German), and Northwestern (Netherlandic–Northern German) dialects. *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* classifies Western Yiddish as endangered.[[22]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-western-25)

The term "Yiddish" is also used in the adjectival sense, synonymously with "Ashkenazi Jewish", to designate attributes of *[Yiddishkeit](/wiki/Yiddishkeit "Yiddishkeit")* ("Ashkenazi culture"; for example, [Yiddish cooking](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine#Jewish_cuisine_variations "Jewish cuisine") and [music](/wiki/Klezmer "Klezmer")).[[23]](#cite_note-26)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

By the 10th century, a distinctive Jewish culture had formed in Central Europe.[[24]](#cite_note-kriwaczek-27): 151 By the [high medieval period](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), their area of settlement, centered on the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland") ([Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz")) and the [Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_(region) "Palatinate (region)") (notably [Worms](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany") and [Speyer](/wiki/Speyer "Speyer")), came to be known as *[Ashkenaz](/wiki/Ashkenaz "Ashkenaz")*,[[25]](#cite_note-28) a term also used for [Scythia](/wiki/Scythia "Scythia"), and later of various areas of Eastern Europe and Anatolia. In the [medieval Hebrew](/wiki/Medieval_Hebrew "Medieval Hebrew") of [Rashi](/wiki/Rashi "Rashi") (d. 1105), *Ashkenaz* becomes a term for Germany, and אשכּנזי *[Ashkenazi](/wiki/Ashkenazi "Ashkenazi")* for the Jews settling in this area.[[24]](#cite_note-kriwaczek-27): Chapter 3, endnote 9[*[need quotation to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[26]](#cite_note-29)
Ashkenaz bordered on the area inhabited by another distinctive Jewish cultural group, the [Sephardi Jews](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews"), who ranged into [southern France](/wiki/Southern_France "Southern France"). Ashkenazi culture later spread into Eastern Europe with large-scale population migrations.[[27]](#cite_note-30)

Nothing is known with certainty about the vernacular of the earliest Jews in Germany, but several theories have been put forward. As noted above, the first language of the Ashkenazim may have been [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"), the vernacular of the Jews in [Roman-era Judea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)") and ancient and early medieval [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"). The widespread use of Aramaic among the large non-Jewish [Syrian](/wiki/Syrians "Syrians") trading population of the Roman provinces, including those in Europe, would have reinforced the use of Aramaic among Jews engaged in trade. In Roman times, many of the Jews living in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") appear to have been [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language")-speakers, and this is reflected in some Ashkenazi personal names (such as *Kalonymos* and Yiddish *Todres*). Hebrew, on the other hand, was regarded as a holy language reserved for ritual and spiritual purposes and not for common use.

The established view is that, as with other [Jewish languages](/wiki/Jewish_languages "Jewish languages"), Jews speaking distinct languages learned new co-territorial vernaculars, which they then Judaized. In the case of Yiddish, this scenario sees it as emerging when speakers of [Zarphatic](/wiki/Zarphatic_language "Zarphatic language") (Judeo-French) and other Judeo-Romance languages began to acquire varieties of [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German"), and from these groups the Ashkenazi community took shape.[[28]](#cite_note-ELL-31)[[29]](#cite_note-Spolsky-32) Exactly what German substrate underlies the earliest form of Yiddish is disputed. The Jewish community in the Rhineland would have encountered the Middle High German dialects from which the [Rhenish German](/wiki/Rhenish_Franconian_languages "Rhenish Franconian languages") dialects of the modern period would emerge. Jewish communities of the high medieval period would have been speaking their own versions of these German dialects, mixed with linguistic elements that they themselves brought into the region, including many Hebrew and Aramaic words, but there is also Romance.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

In [Max Weinreich](/wiki/Max_Weinreich "Max Weinreich")'s model, Jewish speakers of [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") or [Old Italian](/wiki/Old_Italian "Old Italian") who were literate in either liturgical [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") or Aramaic, or both, migrated through Southern Europe to settle in the [Rhine Valley](/wiki/Rhine_Valley "Rhine Valley") in an area known as [Lotharingia](/wiki/Lotharingia "Lotharingia") (later known in Yiddish as *Loter*) extending over parts of Germany and France.[[31]](#cite_note-34) There, they encountered and were influenced by Jewish speakers of [High German languages](/wiki/High_German_languages "High German languages") and several other German dialects. Both Weinreich and [Solomon Birnbaum](/wiki/Solomon_Birnbaum "Solomon Birnbaum") developed this model further in the mid-1950s.[[32]](#cite_note-35) In Weinreich's view, this Old Yiddish substrate later bifurcated into two distinct versions of the language, Western and Eastern Yiddish.[[33]](#cite_note-Fleischer-36) They retained the Semitic vocabulary and constructions needed for religious purposes and created a Judeo-German form of speech, sometimes not accepted as a fully autonomous language.

> Yiddish was a rich, living language, the chattering tongue of an urban population. It had the limitations of its origins. There were few Yiddish words for animals and birds. It had virtually no military vocabulary. Such voids were filled by borrowing from [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"). Yiddish was particularly good at borrowing: from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), from [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), from [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") and from anything with which it intersected. On the other hand, it [contributed](/wiki/Yiddishisms "Yiddishisms") to [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") — [American](/wiki/American_English "American English"). [sic] Its chief virtue lay in its internal subtlety, particularly in its characterization of human types and emotions. It was the language of street wisdom, of the clever underdog, of pathos, resignation and suffering, all of which it palliated by humor, intense irony and superstition. [Isaac Bashevis Singer](/wiki/Isaac_Bashevis_Singer "Isaac Bashevis Singer"), its greatest practitioner, pointed out that it is the only language never spoken by men in power.

– [Paul Johnson](/wiki/Paul_Johnson_(writer) "Paul Johnson (writer)"), *A History of the Jews* (1988)[[34]](#cite_note-37)

Later linguistic research has refined the Weinreich model or provided alternative approaches to the language's origins, with points of contention being the characterization of its Germanic base, the source of its Hebrew/Aramaic [adstrata](/wiki/Stratum_(linguistics) "Stratum (linguistics)"), and the means and location of this fusion. Some theorists argue that the fusion occurred with a Bavarian dialect base.[[29]](#cite_note-Spolsky-32)[[35]](#cite_note-jacobs2005-38): 9–15
The two main candidates for the germinal matrix of Yiddish, the Rhineland and Bavaria, are not necessarily incompatible. There may have been parallel developments in the two regions, seeding the Western and Eastern dialects of Modern Yiddish. [Dovid Katz](/wiki/Dovid_Katz "Dovid Katz") proposes that Yiddish emerged from contact between speakers of High German and Aramaic-speaking Jews from the Middle East.[[17]](#cite_note-yivoyiddish-19) The lines of development proposed by the different theories do not necessarily rule out the others (at least not entirely); an article in *[The Forward](/wiki/The_Forward "The Forward")* argues that "in the end, a new 'standard theory' of Yiddish's origins will probably be based on the work of Weinreich and his challengers alike."[[36]](#cite_note-39)

[Paul Wexler](/wiki/Paul_Wexler_(linguist) "Paul Wexler (linguist)") proposed a model in 1991 that took Yiddish, by which he means primarily eastern Yiddish,[[33]](#cite_note-Fleischer-36) not to be genetically grounded in a Germanic language at all, but rather as "[Judeo-Sorbian](/wiki/Judeo-Sorbian "Judeo-Sorbian")" (a proposed [West Slavic language](/wiki/West_Slavic_language "West Slavic language")) that had been [relexified](/wiki/Relexification "Relexification") by High German.[[29]](#cite_note-Spolsky-32) In more recent work, Wexler has argued that Eastern Yiddish is unrelated genetically to Western Yiddish. Wexler's model has been met with little academic support, and strong critical challenges, especially among historical linguists.[[29]](#cite_note-Spolsky-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Fleischer-36)

### Written evidence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Written evidence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Machzor.jpg/500px-Machzor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Machzor.jpg)

The calligraphic segment in the [Worms](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany") *Machzor* (a Hebrew prayer book). The Yiddish text is in red.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/S%C3%BCd-West-Jiddische_Lebensbeschreibung.jpg/250px-S%C3%BCd-West-Jiddische_Lebensbeschreibung.jpg)](/wiki/File:S%C3%BCd-West-Jiddische_Lebensbeschreibung.jpg)

The South-West Yiddish account of the life of Seligmann Brunschwig von [Dürmenach](/wiki/Durmenach "Durmenach") describes, among other things, the anti-Semitic events of the [revolutionary year 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848"). In the collection of the [Jewish Museum of Switzerland](/wiki/Jewish_Museum_of_Switzerland "Jewish Museum of Switzerland").

[Yiddish orthography](/wiki/Yiddish_orthography "Yiddish orthography") developed towards the end of the High Medieval period. It is first recorded in 1272, with the oldest surviving literary document in Yiddish, a blessing found in the Worms *[machzor](/wiki/Machzor "Machzor")* (a Hebrew prayer book).[[37]](#cite_note-40)[[38]](#cite_note-41)[[14]](#cite_note-baumgarten-16)

A Yiddish phrase transliterated and translated

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Yiddish | גוּט טַק אִים בְּטַגְֿא שְ וַיר דִּיש מַחֲזוֹר אִין בֵּיתֿ הַכְּנֶסֶתֿ טְרַגְֿא |
| Transliterated | *gut tak im betage se vaer dis makhazor in beis hakneses trage* |
| Translated | May a good day come to him who carries this prayer book into the synagogue. |

This brief rhyme is decoratively embedded in an otherwise purely Hebrew text.[[39]](#cite_note-42) Nonetheless, it indicates that the Yiddish of that day was a more or less regular Middle High German written in the Hebrew alphabet into which Hebrew words – מַחֲזוֹר, *[makhzor](/wiki/Machzor "Machzor")* (prayerbook for the [High Holy Days](/wiki/High_Holy_Days "High Holy Days")) and בֵּיתֿ הַכְּנֶסֶתֿ, 'synagogue' (read in Yiddish as *beis hakneses*) – had been included. The [niqqud](/wiki/Niqqud "Niqqud") appears as though it might have been added by a second scribe, in which case it may need to be dated separately and may not be indicative of the pronunciation of the rhyme at the time of its initial annotation.

Over the 14th and 15th centuries, songs and poems in Yiddish, and [macaronic](/wiki/Macaronic_language "Macaronic language") pieces in Hebrew and German, began to appear. These were collected in the late 15th century by Menahem ben Naphtali Oldendorf.[[40]](#cite_note-43) During the same period, a tradition seems to have emerged of the Jewish community's adapting its own versions of German secular literature. The earliest Yiddish epic poem of this sort is the *[Dukus Horant](/wiki/Dukus_Horant "Dukus Horant")*, which survives in the famous Cambridge Codex T.-S.10.K.22. This 14th-century manuscript was discovered in the [Cairo Geniza](/wiki/Cairo_Geniza "Cairo Geniza") in 1896, and also contains a collection of narrative poems on themes from the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") and the [Haggadah](/wiki/Haggadah "Haggadah").

### Printing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Printing")]

The advent of the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") in the 16th century enabled the large-scale production of works at a cheaper cost, some of which have survived. One particularly popular work was [Elia Levita](/wiki/Elia_Levita "Elia Levita")'s *[Bovo-Bukh](/wiki/Bovo-Bukh "Bovo-Bukh")* (בָּבָֿא-בּוך), composed around 1507–08 and printed several times, beginning in 1541 (under the title *Bovo d'Antona*). Levita, the earliest named Yiddish author, may also have written פּאַריז און װיענע *Pariz un Viene* (*Paris and [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna")*). Another Yiddish retelling of a chivalric romance, װידװילט *Vidvilt* (often referred to as "Widuwilt" by Germanizing scholars), presumably also dates from the 15th century, although the manuscripts are from the 16th. It is also known as *Kinig Artus Hof*, an adaptation of the Middle High German romance *Wigalois* by [Wirnt von Grafenberg](/wiki/Wirnt_von_Grafenberg "Wirnt von Grafenberg").[[41]](#cite_note-44) Another significant writer is Avroham ben Schemuel Pikartei, who published a paraphrase on the [Book of Job](/wiki/Book_of_Job "Book of Job") in 1557.

Women in the Ashkenazi community were traditionally not literate in Hebrew but did read and write Yiddish. A body of literature therefore developed for which women were a primary audience. This included secular works, such as the *Bovo-Bukh*, and religious writing specifically for women, such as the צאנה וראינה *[Tseno Ureno](/wiki/Tseno_Ureno "Tseno Ureno")* and the תחנות *[Tkhines](/wiki/Tkhine "Tkhine")*. One of the best-known early woman authors was [Glückel of Hameln](/wiki/Gl%C3%BCckel_of_Hameln "Glückel of Hameln"), whose memoirs are still in print.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Page_from_Yiddish-Hebrew-Latin-German_dictionary_by_Elijah_Levita.jpg/250px-Page_from_Yiddish-Hebrew-Latin-German_dictionary_by_Elijah_Levita.jpg)](/wiki/File:Page_from_Yiddish-Hebrew-Latin-German_dictionary_by_Elijah_Levita.jpg)

A page from the *Shemot Devarim* (lit. 'Names of Things'), a Yiddish–Hebrew–Latin–German dictionary and thesaurus, published by Elia Levita in 1542

The segmentation of the Yiddish readership, between women who read מאַמע־לשון *mame-loshn* but not לשון־קדש *loshn-koydesh*, and men who read both, was significant enough that distinctive [typefaces](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface") were used for each. The name commonly given to the semicursive form used exclusively for Yiddish was ווײַבערטײַטש (*[vaybertaytsh](/wiki/Vaybertaytsh "Vaybertaytsh")*, 'women's *taytsh*', shown in the heading and fourth column in the *[Shemot Devarim](/wiki/Shemot_Devarim "Shemot Devarim")*), with square Hebrew letters (shown in the third column) being reserved for text in that language and Aramaic. This distinction was retained in general typographic practice through to the early 19th century, with Yiddish books being set in *vaybertaytsh* (also termed מעשייט *mesheyt* or מאַשקעט *mashket*—the construction is uncertain).[[42]](#cite_note-45)

An additional distinctive semicursive typeface was, and still is, used for rabbinical commentary on religious texts when Hebrew and Yiddish appear on the same page. This is commonly termed [Rashi script](/wiki/Rashi_script "Rashi script"), from the name of the most renowned early author, whose commentary is usually printed using this script. (Rashi is also the typeface normally used when the Sephardic counterpart to Yiddish, [Judeo-Spanish](/wiki/Judeo-Spanish "Judeo-Spanish") or *Ladino*, is printed in Hebrew script.)

According to a study by the German media association Internationale Medienhilfe (IMH), more than 40 printed Yiddish newspapers and magazines were published worldwide in 2024, and the trend is rising.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

### Secularization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Secularization")]

The Western Yiddish dialect—sometimes pejoratively labeled *Mauscheldeutsch*,[[44]](#cite_note-47) i. e. "Moses German"[[45]](#cite_note-48)—declined in the 18th century, as the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") and the *[Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")* led to a view of Yiddish as a corrupt dialect. The 19th-century Prussian-Jewish historian [Heinrich Graetz](/wiki/Heinrich_Graetz "Heinrich Graetz"), for example, wrote that "the language of the Jews [in Poland] ... degenerat[ed] into a ridiculous jargon, a mixture of German, Polish, and Talmudical elements, an unpleasant stammering, rendered still more repulsive by forced attempts at wit."[[46]](#cite_note-49)

A *[Maskil](/wiki/Maskil "Maskil")* (one who takes part in the *Haskalah*) would write about and promote acclimatization to the outside world.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-50) Jewish children began attending secular schools where the primary language spoken and taught was German, not Yiddish.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-50)[[48]](#cite_note-51)

> Yiddish grates on our ears and distorts. This jargon is incapable in fact of expressing sublime thoughts. It is our obligation to cast off these old rags, a heritage of the dark Middle Ages.

– [Osip Aronovich Rabinovich](/wiki/Osip_Rabinovich "Osip Rabinovich"), in an article titled "Russia – Our Native Land: Just as We Breathe Its Air, We Must Speak Its Language" in the [Odessan](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa") journal *Рассвет* (dawn), 1861[[49]](#cite_note-52)

Owing to both assimilation to German and the [revival of Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"), Western Yiddish survived only as a language of "intimate family circles or of closely knit trade groups".[[50]](#cite_note-53)

In Eastern Europe, the response to these forces took the opposite direction, with Yiddish becoming the cohesive force in a [secular culture](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") (see the [Yiddishist movement](/wiki/Yiddishist_movement "Yiddishist movement")). Notable Yiddish writers of the late 19th and early 20th centuries are Sholem Yankev Abramovitch, writing as [Mendele Mocher Sforim](/wiki/Mendele_Mocher_Sforim "Mendele Mocher Sforim"); Sholem Rabinovitsh, widely known as [Sholem Aleichem](/wiki/Sholem_Aleichem "Sholem Aleichem"), whose stories about טבֿיה דער מילכיקער (*Tevye der milkhiker*, "[Tevye](/wiki/Tevye "Tevye") the Milkman") inspired the Broadway musical and film *[Fiddler on the Roof](/wiki/Fiddler_on_the_Roof "Fiddler on the Roof")*; and [Isaac Leib Peretz](/wiki/I._L._Peretz "I. L. Peretz").

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Yiddish_WWI_poster2.jpg/250px-Yiddish_WWI_poster2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yiddish_WWI_poster2.jpg)

American [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")-era poster in Yiddish. Translated caption: "Food will win the war – [You came here seeking freedom](/wiki/History_of_immigration_to_the_United_States#New_immigration "History of immigration to the United States"), now you must help to preserve it – We must supply the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") with wheat – Let nothing go to waste". Color lithograph, 1917. Digitally restored.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Miensk_Railway_Station_1926.jpg/250px-Miensk_Railway_Station_1926.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miensk_Railway_Station_1926.jpg)

Minsk railway station in 1926. Four languages: [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), and Yiddish.

In the early 20th century, especially after the Socialist [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") in Russia, Yiddish was emerging as a major Eastern European language. Its rich literature was more widely published than ever, [Yiddish theatre](/wiki/Yiddish_theatre "Yiddish theatre") and [Yiddish cinema](/wiki/Yiddish_cinema "Yiddish cinema") were booming, and for a time it achieved the status of one of the [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the short-lived [Galician Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Galician_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Galician Soviet Socialist Republic"). Educational autonomy for Jews in several countries (notably [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland")) after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") led to an increase in formal Yiddish-language education, more uniform orthography, and to the 1925 founding of the Yiddish Scientific Institute, [YIVO](/wiki/YIVO "YIVO"). In [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius"), there was debate over which language should take primacy, Hebrew or Yiddish.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

Yiddish changed significantly during the 20th century. [Michael Wex](/wiki/Michael_Wex "Michael Wex") writes, "As increasing numbers of Yiddish speakers moved from the Slavic-speaking East to Western Europe and the Americas in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, they were so quick to jettison Slavic vocabulary that the most prominent Yiddish writers of the time—the founders of modern Yiddish literature, who were still living in Slavic-speaking countries—revised the printed editions of their oeuvres to eliminate obsolete and 'unnecessary' Slavisms."[[52]](#cite_note-55) The vocabulary used in Israel absorbed many Modern Hebrew words, and there was a similar but smaller increase in the English component of Yiddish in the United States and, to a lesser extent, the United Kingdom.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This has resulted in some difficulty in communication between Yiddish speakers from Israel and those from other countries.

"[Khurbn](/wiki/Khurbn "Khurbn") Yiddish", as discussed by Professor Hannah Pollin-Galay, refers to the [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") shaped by Yiddish speakers' experience during the Holocaust. Prisoners developed new words and slang, particularly relating to theft, protest, and sexuality.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Yiddish phonology](/wiki/Yiddish_phonology "Yiddish phonology")

There is significant [phonological](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") variation among the various [Yiddish dialects](/wiki/Yiddish_dialects "Yiddish dialects"). The description that follows is of a modern Standard Yiddish that was devised during the early 20th century and is frequently encountered in pedagogical contexts.

  + [ɣ] is an allophone of /χ/ before /b, d, ɡ, v, z, ʒ/.[[55]](#cite_note-katz1987-58)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
* The phonetic realization of /χ/ and /nʲ/ is unclear:
  + In the case of /χ/, [Kleine (2003)](#CITEREFKleine2003) puts it in the "velar" column, but consistently uses a symbol denoting a voiceless *uvular* fricative ⟨χ⟩ to transcribe it.
  + In the case of /nʲ/, [Kleine (2003)](#CITEREFKleine2003) puts it in the "palatalized" column. This can mean that it is either palatalized alveolar [nʲ] or alveolo-palatal [[ɲ̟](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_nasal "Voiced alveolo-palatal nasal")]. /ʎ/ may actually also be alveolo-palatal [[ʎ̟](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolo-palatal lateral approximant")], rather than just palatal.
* The rhotic /r/ can be either alveolar or uvular, either a trill [[r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill") ~ [ʀ](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_trill "Voiced uvular trill")] or, more commonly, a flap/tap [[ɾ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_tap_or_flap "Voiced alveolar tap or flap") ~ [ʀ̆](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_tap_and_flap "Voiced uvular tap and flap")].[[54]](#cite_note-kleine-57)
* The glottal stop [ʔ] appears only as an intervocalic separator.[[54]](#cite_note-kleine-57)

As in the [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") with which Yiddish was long in [contact](/wiki/Language_contact "Language contact") (Russian, [Belarusian](/wiki/Belarusian_language "Belarusian language"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language")), but unlike German, [voiceless](/wiki/Voiceless_consonant "Voiceless consonant") stops have little to no [aspiration](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)"); unlike many such languages, voiced stops are not devoiced in final position.[[54]](#cite_note-kleine-57) Moreover, Yiddish has regressive [voicing](/wiki/Phonation "Phonation") [assimilation](/wiki/Assimilation_(linguistics) "Assimilation (linguistics)"), so that, for example, זאָגט /zɔɡt/ ('says') is pronounced [zɔkt] and הקדמה /hakˈdɔmɜ/ ('foreword') is pronounced [haɡˈdɔmɜ].

In addition, the [sonorants](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant") /l/ and /n/ can function as [syllable nuclei](/wiki/Syllable#Nucleus "Syllable"):

[m] and [ŋ] appear as syllable nuclei as well, but only as allophones of /n/, after bilabial consonants and [dorsal consonants](/wiki/Dorsal_consonant "Dorsal consonant"), respectively.

The syllabic sonorants are always unstressed.

### Dialectal variation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dialectal variation")]

Stressed vowels in the [Yiddish dialects](/wiki/Yiddish_dialects "Yiddish dialects") may be understood by considering their common origins in the Proto-Yiddish sound system. Yiddish linguistic scholarship uses a system developed by [Max Weinreich](/wiki/Max_Weinreich "Max Weinreich") in 1960 to indicate the descendent [diaphonemes](/wiki/Diaphoneme "Diaphoneme") of the Proto-Yiddish stressed vowels.[[35]](#cite_note-jacobs2005-38): 28

Each Proto-Yiddish vowel is given a unique two-digit identifier, and its reflexes use it as a subscript, for example, Southeastern *o11* is the vowel /o/, descended from Proto-Yiddish \*/a/.[[35]](#cite_note-jacobs2005-38): 28 The first digit indicates Proto-Yiddish *quality* (1-=\*[a], 2-=\*[e], 3-=\*[i], 4-=\*[o], 5-=\*[u]), and the second refers to *quantity or diphthongization* (−1=short, −2=long, −3=short but lengthened early in the history of Yiddish, −4=diphthong, −5=special length occurring only in Proto-Yiddish vowel 25).[[35]](#cite_note-jacobs2005-38): 28

Vowels 23, 33, 43 and 53 have the same reflexes as 22, 32, 42 and 52 in all Yiddish dialects, but they developed distinct values in [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German"); Katz (1987) argues that they should be collapsed with the −2 series, leaving only 13 in the −3 series.[[55]](#cite_note-katz1987-58): 17

### Comparison with German

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Comparison with German")]

See also: [High German consonant shift](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift")

In the vocabulary of Germanic origin, the differences between Standard German and Yiddish pronunciation are mainly in the [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") and [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"). All varieties of Yiddish lack the German [front rounded vowels](/wiki/Front_rounded_vowel "Front rounded vowel") /œ, øː/ and /ʏ, yː/, having merged them with /ɛ, e:/ and /ɪ, i:/, respectively. In many respects, particularly with vowels and vowel diphthongs, and even how it forms diminutives, Yiddish is closer to [Swabian German](/wiki/Swabian_German "Swabian German") than to standard High German.

Diphthongs have also undergone divergent developments in German and Yiddish. Where Standard German has merged the [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") diphthong *ei* and long vowel *î* to /aɪ/, Yiddish has maintained the distinction between them; and likewise, the Standard German /ɔʏ/ corresponds to both the MHG diphthong *öu* and the long vowel *iu*, which in Yiddish have merged with their unrounded counterparts *ei* and *î*, respectively. Lastly, the Standard German /aʊ/ corresponds to both the MHG diphthong *ou* and the long vowel *û*, but in Yiddish, they have not merged. Although Standard Yiddish does not distinguish between those two diphthongs and renders both as /ɔɪ/, the distinction becomes apparent when the two diphthongs undergo [Germanic umlaut](/wiki/Germanic_umlaut "Germanic umlaut"), such as in forming plurals:

| Singular | | | | Plural | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| English | MHG | Standard German | Standard Yiddish | Standard German | Standard Yiddish |

| abdomen | *bûch* | *Bauch* /baʊ̯x/ | בױך /bɔɪχ/ | *Bäuche* /ˈbɔʏ̯çə/ | בײַכער‎ /baɪχɜr/ |

The [vowel length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") distinctions of German do not exist in the Northeastern (Lithuanian) varieties of Yiddish, which form the phonetic basis for Standard Yiddish. In those varieties, the vowel qualities in most long/short vowel pairs diverged, and so the phonemic distinction has remained.

There are consonantal differences between German and Yiddish. Yiddish [deaffricates](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") the Middle High German [voiceless labiodental affricate](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_affricate "Voiceless labiodental affricate") /pf/ to /f/ initially (as in פֿונט *funt*, but this pronunciation is also quasi-standard throughout northern and central Germany); /pf/ surfaces as an [unshifted](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift") /p/ medially or finally (as in עפּל /ɛpl/ and קאָפּ /kɔp/). Additionally, final voiced stops appear in Standard Yiddish but not in Northern Standard German.

The pronunciation of vowels in Yiddish words of [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") origin is similar to [Ashkenazi Hebrew](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Hebrew "Ashkenazi Hebrew") but not identical. The most prominent difference is *[kamatz](/wiki/Kamatz "Kamatz") gadol* in closed syllables being pronounced the same as *[patah](/wiki/Patah "Patah")* in Yiddish but the same as any other *kamatz* in Ashkenazi Hebrew. Also, Hebrew features no reduction of unstressed vowels, and so the given name [Jochebed](/wiki/Jochebed "Jochebed") יוֹכֶבֶֿד would be /jɔɪˈχɛvɛd/ in Ashkenazi Hebrew but /ˈjɔχvɜd/ in Standard Yiddish.

[Yiddish grammar](/wiki/Yiddish_grammar "Yiddish grammar") can vary slightly depending on the dialect. The main article focuses on standard form of Yiddish grammar while also acknowledging some dialectal differences. Yiddish grammar has similarities to the German grammar system, as well as grammatical elements from Hebrew and Slavic languages.

## Writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Writing system")]

Main article: [Yiddish orthography](/wiki/Yiddish_orthography "Yiddish orthography")

Yiddish is written in the [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet"), but its [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") differs significantly from [that of Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_spelling "Hebrew spelling"). In Hebrew, many vowels are represented only optionally by [diacritical marks](/wiki/Diacritical_marks "Diacritical marks") called [niqqud](/wiki/Niqqud "Niqqud") whereas Yiddish uses letters to represent all vowels. Several Yiddish letters consist of another letter combined with a niqqud mark resembling a Hebrew letter–niqqud pair, but each of those combinations is an inseparable unit representing a vowel alone, not a consonant–vowel sequence. The niqqud marks have no phonetic value on their own.

In most varieties of Yiddish, however, words borrowed from Hebrew are written in their native forms without application of Yiddish orthographical conventions.

## Numbers of speakers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Numbers of speakers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Yiddish.png/250px-Yiddish.png)](/wiki/File:Yiddish.png)

Map of the Yiddish dialects between the 15th and the 19th centuries (Western dialects in orange / Eastern dialects in green)

> Ghosts love Yiddish and as far as I know, they all speak it.

– [Isaac Bashevis Singer](/wiki/Isaac_Bashevis_Singer "Isaac Bashevis Singer") upon receiving the [Nobel Prize for Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_for_Literature "Nobel Prize for Literature"), 1978[[56]](#cite_note-59)

On the eve of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), there were 11 to 13 million Yiddish speakers.[[17]](#cite_note-yivoyiddish-19) [The Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"), however, led to a dramatic, sudden decline in the use of Yiddish, as the extensive Jewish communities, both secular and religious, that used Yiddish in their day-to-day life were largely destroyed. Around five million of those killed – 85 percent of the Jews murdered in the Holocaust – were speakers of Yiddish.[[19]](#cite_note-Sprache_1984_p._3-21) Although millions of Yiddish speakers survived the war (including nearly all Yiddish speakers in the Americas), further assimilation in countries such as the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), in addition to the strictly monolingual stance of the [Haskalah](/wiki/Haskalah "Haskalah")[[57]](#cite_note-60) and later [Zionist](/wiki/Zionist "Zionist") movements, led to a decline in the use of Eastern Yiddish.[[58]](#cite_note-auto-61) However, the number of speakers within the widely dispersed Haredi (mainly Hasidic) communities is now increasing. Although used in various countries, Yiddish has attained official recognition as a [minority language](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") only in the [Jewish Autonomous Oblast](/wiki/Jewish_Autonomous_Oblast "Jewish Autonomous Oblast") of Russia, [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"),[[59]](#cite_note-62) and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").

Reports of the number of current Yiddish speakers vary significantly. *[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* estimates, based on publications through 1991, that there were at that time 1.5 million speakers of Eastern Yiddish,[[60]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-63) of which 40% lived in Ukraine, 15% in Israel, and 10% in the United States. The [Modern Language Association](/wiki/Modern_Language_Association "Modern Language Association") agrees with fewer than 200,000 in the United States.[[61]](#cite_note-64) Western Yiddish is reported by *Ethnologue* to have had an ethnic population of 50,000 in 2000, and an undated speaking population of 5,400, mostly in Germany.[[22]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-western-25)[[62]](#cite_note-65) A 1996 report by the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") estimates a worldwide Yiddish-speaking population of about two million.[[63]](#cite_note-66) Further [demographic](/wiki/Demographics "Demographics") information about the recent status of what is treated as an Eastern–Western dialect continuum is provided in the YIVO *Language and Cultural Atlas of Ashkenazic Jewry*.

In a study in the first half of 2024, the German media association Internationale Medienhilfe (IMH) found that the number of Yiddish media is increasing again, due to an increase in the Yiddish-speaking population, especially in the USA. According to IMH estimates, the number of speakers worldwide is approaching two million. In 2024, more than 40 print media were published worldwide in Yiddish - and the trend is rising.[[64]](#cite_note-67)

The [1922 census of Palestine](/wiki/1922_census_of_Palestine "1922 census of Palestine") lists 1,946 Yiddish speakers in Mandatory Palestine (9 in the Southern District, 1,401 in Jerusalem-Jaffa, 4 in Samaria, and 532 in the Northern District), including 1,759 in municipal areas (999 in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), 356 in [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa"), 332 in [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"), 5 in [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"), 4 in [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron"), 3 in [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth"), 7 in [Ramleh](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla"), 33 in [Tiberias](/wiki/Tiberias "Tiberias"), and 4 in [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin")).[[65]](#cite_note-68)

In the Hasidic communities of Israel, boys speak more Yiddish amongst themselves, while girls use Hebrew more often. This is probably due to the tendency of girls to learn more secular subjects, thus increasing their contact with the Hebrew language, while boys are usually taught religious subjects in Yiddish.[[66]](#cite_note-69)

## Status as a language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Status as a language")]

Historically, there have been frequent debates about the extent of the linguistic independence of Yiddish from the languages that it absorbed. There has been periodic assertion that Yiddish is a dialect of German, or even "just broken German, more of a linguistic mishmash than a true language".[[67]](#cite_note-NYT_Johnson-70) Even when recognized as an autonomous language, it has occasionally been referred to, typically by people foreign to the language, as Judeo-German, along the lines of other Jewish languages like [Judeo-Persian](/wiki/Judeo-Persian "Judeo-Persian"), [Judeo-Spanish](/wiki/Judeo-Spanish "Judeo-Spanish") or [Judeo-French](/wiki/Judeo-French "Judeo-French"). A widely cited summary of attitudes in the 1930s was published by [Max Weinreich](/wiki/Max_Weinreich "Max Weinreich"), quoting a remark by an auditor of one of his lectures: אַ שפּראַך איז אַ דיאַלעקט מיט אַן אַרמיי און פֿלאָט (*a shprakh iz a dialekt mit an armey un flot*[[68]](#cite_note-71) — "[A language is a dialect with an army and navy](/wiki/A_language_is_a_dialect_with_an_army_and_navy "A language is a dialect with an army and navy")").

Today's speakers consider Yiddish a separate language, officially recognized as such in the USSR (where it was viewed as "the Jewish language"), [post-Soviet Russia](/wiki/Jewish_Autonomous_Oblast "Jewish Autonomous Oblast") and Sweden, thus complying to Max Weinreich's notion of official state recognition. Virtually all specialists working in the field of Yiddish view it as a separate language, including researchers and teachers who study and teach Yiddish in German-speaking countries. For centuries, Yiddish has been developing in countries separated from the German language space and has its own system of dialects. Contemporary debates on this subject are almost exclusively limited to the nature of medieval and early modern texts written in Western Yiddish dialects that seem much closer to varieties of German than today's Eastern Yiddish.[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-73)

### Israel and Zionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Israel and Zionism")]

> We shall get rid of the stunted and squashed jargons which we use now, these ghetto languages. They were the furtive tongues of prisoners.[[d]](#cite_note-74)

— [Theodor Herzl](/wiki/Theodor_Herzl "Theodor Herzl"), *[Der Judenstaat](/wiki/Der_Judenstaat "Der Judenstaat")*, 1896.[[71]](#cite_note-75)

See also: [War of the Languages](/wiki/War_of_the_Languages "War of the Languages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Yidish_graffiti.JPG/250px-Yidish_graffiti.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yidish_graffiti.JPG)

An example of graffiti in Yiddish, Tel Aviv, Washington Avenue (און איר זאלט ליב האבן דעם פרעמדען, ווארום פרעמדע זייט איר געווען אין לאנד מצרים—*Un ir zolt lib hobn dem fremden, varum fremde zayt ir geven in land mitsraym*). "You shall have love for the stranger, because you were strangers in the land of Egypt." (Deuteronomy 10:19)

The national language of Israel is [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"). The debate in Zionist circles over the use of Yiddish in Israel and in the diaspora in preference to Hebrew also reflected the tensions between religious and secular Jewish lifestyles. Many secular Zionists wanted Hebrew as the sole language of Jews, to contribute to a national cohesive identity. Traditionally religious Jews, on the other hand, preferred use of Yiddish, viewing Hebrew as a respected holy language reserved for prayer and religious study. In the early 20th century, Zionist activists in the [Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") tried to eradicate the use of Yiddish among Jews in preference to Hebrew, and make its use socially unacceptable.[[72]](#cite_note-76)

This conflict also reflected the opposing views among secular Jews worldwide, one side seeing Hebrew (and Zionism) and the other Yiddish (and [Internationalism](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)")) as the means of defining Jewish nationalism. In the 1920s and 1930s, גדוד מגיני השפה *gdud maginéi hasafá*, "[Battalion for the Defence of the Language](/wiki/Battalion_for_the_Defence_of_the_Language "Battalion for the Defence of the Language")", whose motto was "עברי, דבר עברית *ivri, dabér ivrít*", that is, "Hebrew [i.e. Jew], speak Hebrew!", used to tear down signs written in "foreign" languages and disturb Yiddish theatre gatherings with stink bombs.[[73]](#cite_note-77) In 1927, a proposal to institute a chair in Yiddish at [Hebrew University](/wiki/Hebrew_University "Hebrew University") was met with protests.[[58]](#cite_note-auto-61) However, according to linguist [Ghil'ad Zuckermann](/wiki/Ghil%27ad_Zuckermann "Ghil'ad Zuckermann"), the members of this group in particular, and the Hebrew revival in general, did not succeed in uprooting Yiddish patterns (as well as the patterns of other European languages Jewish immigrants spoke) within what he calls "Israeli", i.e. [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"). Zuckermann believes that "Israeli does include numerous Hebrew elements resulting from a conscious revival but also numerous pervasive linguistic features deriving from a subconscious survival of the revivalists’ mother tongues, e.g. Yiddish."[[74]](#cite_note-78)

After the founding of the State of Israel, a massive wave of [Jewish immigrants from Arab countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_Arab_and_Muslim_countries "Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries") arrived. In short order, these [Mizrahi Jews](/wiki/Mizrahi_Jews "Mizrahi Jews") and their descendants would account for nearly half the Jewish population. While all were at least familiar with Hebrew as a liturgical language, essentially none had any contact with or affinity for Yiddish (some, of [Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews") origin, spoke Judeo-Spanish, others various [Judeo-Arabic varieties](/wiki/Judeo-Arabic_dialects "Judeo-Arabic dialects")). Thus, Hebrew emerged as the dominant linguistic common denominator between the different population groups.

According to Itay Zutra, a teacher of Yiddish at the [University of Manitoba](/wiki/University_of_Manitoba "University of Manitoba"), Yiddish was portrayed as a feminine and emasculate language in Israel, sometimes even associated with being a homosexual. One such example of this is in the 1970 movie *[Shablul](/wiki/Shablul "Shablul")*, where a frail Yiddish speaker, told he needs to be a strong Jewish man, goes to a karate instructor for help. The instructor, also a Yiddish speaker, agrees to teach him. The instructor then tries to grope and have sex with the man during a private session.[[75]](#cite_note-79) It has also been argued by Yiddish writer and literary critic [Shmuel Niger](/wiki/Shmuel_Niger "Shmuel Niger") and professor [Naomi Seidman](/wiki/Naomi_Seidman "Naomi Seidman") that even before Zionism, Yiddish was seen as having an inherent level of femininity, while Hebrew was the opposite and was inherently masculine. Much of this stems from the fact that women were excluded from having formal training in Hebrew, which made Yiddish the only Jewish language women could speak, read and write in. As a consequence, Yiddish texts and the language as a whole inherently took on feminine traits, even when written and spoken by men.[[76]](#cite_note-80)

Despite a past of marginalization and [anti-Yiddish](/wiki/Anti-Yiddish "Anti-Yiddish") government policy, in 1996 the [Knesset](/wiki/Knesset "Knesset") passed a law founding the "National Authority for Yiddish Culture", with the aim of supporting and promoting contemporary Yiddish art and [literature](/wiki/Yiddish_literature "Yiddish literature"), as well as preservation of [Yiddish culture](/wiki/Yiddish_culture "Yiddish culture") and publication of Yiddish classics, both in Yiddish and in Hebrew translation.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

In religious circles, it is Ashkenazi [Haredi Jews](/wiki/Haredi_Judaism "Haredi Judaism"), particularly Hasidic Jews and the Lithuanian yeshiva world, who continue to teach, speak and use Yiddish (which, in Israel, has evolved into the [Haredi dialect](/wiki/Haredi_dialect "Haredi dialect")), making it a language used regularly by hundreds of thousands of Haredi Jews today. The largest of these centers are in [Bnei Brak](/wiki/Bnei_Brak "Bnei Brak") and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").

There is a growing revival of interest in Yiddish culture among secular Israelis, with the flourishing of new proactive cultural organizations like YUNG YiDiSH, as well as [Yiddish theatre](/wiki/Yiddish_theatre "Yiddish theatre") (usually with simultaneous translation to Hebrew and Russian) and young people are taking university courses in Yiddish, some achieving considerable fluency.[[67]](#cite_note-NYT_Johnson-70)[[78]](#cite_note-82)

### South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: South Africa")]

In the early years of the 20th century, Yiddish was classified as a 'Semitic Language'. After much campaigning, in 1906, the South African legislator [Morris Alexander](/wiki/Morris_Alexander "Morris Alexander") won a parliamentary fight to have Yiddish reclassified as a European language, thereby permitting the immigration of Yiddish speakers to South Africa.[[79]](#cite_note-83) While there used to be a large Yiddish press in South Africa now Yiddish has largely died out in South Africa being replaced with other languages.[[80]](#cite_note-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mexico")]

In [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), Yiddish was spoken among the Ashkenazi Jewish population and Yiddish poet [Isaac Berliner](/wiki/Isaac_Berliner "Isaac Berliner") wrote about the life of [Mexican Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Mexico "History of the Jews in Mexico"). Isaac Berliner's Yiddishism was a way for the Ashkenazi Jews in Mexico to build a secular culture in a Mexico skeptical of religion.[[82]](#cite_note-86) Yiddish became a marker of Ashkenazi ethnic identity in Mexico.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

### Former Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Former Soviet Union")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Drive_to_the_Collective_Farm.jpg/250px-Drive_to_the_Collective_Farm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drive_to_the_Collective_Farm.jpg)

[NEP](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy")-era Soviet Yiddish poster "Come to us at the [Kolkhoz](/wiki/Kolkhoz "Kolkhoz")!" (*קום צו אונדז אין קאָלווירט!*)

In the Soviet Union during the era of the [New Economic Policy](/wiki/New_Economic_Policy "New Economic Policy") (NEP) in the 1920s, Yiddish was promoted as the language of the Jewish [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat"). At the same time, [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language") was considered a [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois") and [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") language and its use was generally discouraged.[[84]](#cite_note-88)[[85]](#cite_note-89) Yiddish was one of the recognized languages of the [Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic"). Until 1938, the [Emblem of the Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Emblem_of_the_Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Emblem of the Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic") included the motto *[Workers of the world, unite!](/wiki/Workers_of_the_world,_unite! "Workers of the world, unite!")* in Yiddish. Yiddish was also an official language in several agricultural districts of the [Galician Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Galician_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Galician Soviet Socialist Republic").

The use of Yiddish as the primary spoken language by Jews was heavily encouraged by multiple Jewish political groups at the time. The [Evsektsii](/wiki/Evsektsiia "Evsektsiia"), the Jewish Communist Group, and The [Bund](/wiki/General_Jewish_Labour_Bund "General Jewish Labour Bund"), the Jewish Socialist Group, both heavily encouraged the use of Yiddish. During the Bolshevik Era these political groups worked alongside the government to encourage the widespread Jewish use of Yiddish. Both the Evsektsii and the Bund supported the Jewish movement towards assimilation and saw Yiddish as a way to encourage it. They saw the use of Yiddish as a step away from the religious aspects of Judaism, instead favoring the cultural aspects of Judaism.[[86]](#cite_note-90)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Emblem_of_the_Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic_%281927%E2%80%931937%29.svg/250px-Emblem_of_the_Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic_%281927%E2%80%931937%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Emblem_of_the_Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic_(1927%E2%80%931937).svg)

State emblem of the [Byelorussian SSR](/wiki/Byelorussian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Byelorussian Soviet Socialist Republic") (1927–1937) with the motto *Workers of the world, unite!* in Yiddish (lower left part of the ribbon): ״פראָלעטאריער פון אלע לענדער, פאראייניקט זיך!״—*Proletarier fun ale lender, fareynikt zikh**!* The same slogan is written in Belarusian, Russian and Polish.

A public educational system entirely based on the Yiddish language was established and comprised kindergartens, schools, and higher educational institutions (technical schools, [rabfaks](/wiki/Rabfak "Rabfak") and other university departments).[[87]](#cite_note-:2-91) These were initially created in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") to stop Jewish children from taking too many spots in regular Russian schools. Imperial government feared that the Jewish children were both taking spots from non-Jews as well as spreading revolutionary ideas to their non-Jewish peers. As a result, in 1914 laws were passed that guaranteed Jews the right to a Jewish education and as a result the Yiddish education system was established.[[88]](#cite_note-92)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] After the Bolshevik revolution in 1917 even more Yiddish schools were established. These schools thrived with government, specifically Bolshevik, and Jewish support. They were established as part of the effort to revitalize the Soviet Jewish Community. Specifically, the Bolsheviks wanted to encourage Jewish assimilation. While these schools were taught in Yiddish, the content was Soviet. They were created to attract Jews in to getting a Soviet education under the guise of a Jewish institution.[[89]](#cite_note-:3-93)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

While schools with curriculums taught in Yiddish existed in some areas until the 1950s, there was a general decline in enrollment due to preference for Russian-speaking institutions and the declining reputation of Yiddish schools among Yiddish speaking Soviets. As the Yiddish schools declined, so did overall Yiddish culture. The two were inherently linked and with the downfall of one, so did the other.[[90]](#cite_note-94)[[89]](#cite_note-:3-93)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] General Soviet denationalization programs and secularization policies also led to a further lack of enrollment and funding; the last schools to be closed existed until 1951.[[87]](#cite_note-:2-91) It continued to be spoken widely for decades, nonetheless, in areas with compact Jewish populations (primarily in Moldova, Ukraine, and to a lesser extent Belarus).

In the former Soviet states, recently active Yiddish authors include [Yoysef Burg](/wiki/Josef_Burg_(writer) "Josef Burg (writer)") ([Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi") 1912–2009) and [Olexander Beyderman](/wiki/Olexander_Beyderman "Olexander Beyderman") (b. 1949, [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa")). Publication of an earlier Yiddish periodical (דער פֿרײַנד – *der fraynd*; lit. *The Friend*), was resumed in 2004 with דער נײַער פֿרײַנד (*der nayer fraynd*; lit. *The New Friend*, [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg")).

#### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main articles: [Jewish Autonomous Oblast](/wiki/Jewish_Autonomous_Oblast "Jewish Autonomous Oblast"), [Birobidzhan](/wiki/Birobidzhan "Birobidzhan"), and [History of the Jews in the Jewish Autonomous Oblast](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_the_Jewish_Autonomous_Oblast "History of the Jews in the Jewish Autonomous Oblast")

According to the [2010 census](/wiki/Russian_Census_(2010) "Russian Census (2010)"), 1,683 people spoke Yiddish in Russia, approximately 1% of all the Jews of the Russian Federation.[[91]](#cite_note-95) According to [Mikhail Shvydkoy](/wiki/Mikhail_Shvydkoy "Mikhail Shvydkoy"), former Minister of Culture of Russia and himself of Jewish origin, Yiddish culture in Russia is gone, and its revival is unlikely.[[92]](#cite_note-Lechaim.ru-96)

> From my point of view, Yiddish culture today isn't just fading away, but disappearing. It is stored as memories, as fragments of phrases, as books that have long gone unread. ... Yiddish culture is dying and this should be treated with utmost calm. There is no need to pity that which cannot be resurrected – it has receded into the world of the enchanting past, where it should remain. Any artificial culture, a culture without replenishment, is meaningless. ... Everything that happens with Yiddish culture is transformed into a kind of cabaret—epistolary genre, nice, cute to the ear and the eye, but having nothing to do with high art, because there is no natural, national soil. In Russia, it is the memory of the departed, sometimes sweet memories. But it's the memories of what will never be again. Perhaps that's why these memories are always so sharp.[[92]](#cite_note-Lechaim.ru-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/RussiaJewish2007-07.png/250px-RussiaJewish2007-07.png)](/wiki/File:RussiaJewish2007-07.png)

The [Jewish Autonomous Oblast](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_the_Jewish_Autonomous_Oblast "History of the Jews in the Jewish Autonomous Oblast") in Russia

The Jewish Autonomous Oblast was formed in 1934 in the [Russian Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East"), with its capital city in Birobidzhan and Yiddish as its official language.[[93]](#cite_note-97) The intention was for the Soviet Jewish population to settle there. Jewish cultural life was revived in Birobidzhan much earlier than elsewhere in the Soviet Union. Yiddish theaters began opening in the 1970s. The newspaper ביראָבידזשאַנער שטערן (*[Birobidzhaner Shtern](/wiki/Birobidzhaner_Shtern "Birobidzhaner Shtern")*; lit: *Birobidzhan Star*) includes a Yiddish section.[[94]](#cite_note-98) In modern Russia, the cultural significance of the language is still recognized and bolstered. The First Birobidzhan International Summer Program for Yiddish Language and Culture was launched in 2007.[[95]](#cite_note-99)

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit), according to data provided by the Russian Census Bureau, there were 97 speakers of Yiddish in the JAO.[[96]](#cite_note-100) A November 2017 article in *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, titled, "Revival of a Soviet Zion: Birobidzhan celebrates its Jewish heritage", examined the current status of the city and suggested that, even though the Jewish Autonomous Region in Russia's far east is now barely 1% Jewish, officials hope to woo back people who left after Soviet collapse and to revive the Yiddish language in this region.[[97]](#cite_note-101) Despite the small number of local speakers, the weekly state-run newspaper *[Birobidzhaner Shtern](/wiki/Birobidzhaner_Shtern "Birobidzhaner Shtern")* contains 2–4 pages in Yiddish, largely written by authors who live in other cities and countries, and its online version attracts international readership. Yiddish often appears in the local TV program Yiddishkeit, also available online.[[98]](#cite_note-102)

#### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

Yiddish was an official language of the [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") (1917–1921).[[99]](#cite_note-Ukraine:_Birth_of_a_Modern_Nation-103)[[100]](#cite_note-HoUJsntui-104) But due to the holocaust, assimilation, and migration of [Ukrainian Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Ukraine "History of the Jews in Ukraine") abroad today only 3,100 of the remaining Jews speak Yiddish as their first language.[[101]](#cite_note-105) The Southeast dialect of Yiddish has many Ukrainian loanwords due to the long contact between Yiddish speakers and Ukrainian speakers.[[102]](#cite_note-106)

### Council of Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Council of Europe")]

Several countries that ratified the 1992 [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages") have included Yiddish in the list of their recognized minority languages: the Netherlands (1996), Sweden (2000), Romania (2008), Poland (2009), Bosnia and Herzegovina (2010).[[103]](#cite_note-:1-107) In 2005, Ukraine did not mention Yiddish as such, but "the language(s) of the Jewish ethnic minority".[[103]](#cite_note-:1-107)

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sweden")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Jidische.Folkschtime.jpg/250px-Jidische.Folkschtime.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jidische.Folkschtime.jpg)

Banner from the first issue of the יודישע פאלקסשטימע—*Jidische Folkschtime* (*Yiddish People's Voice*), published in Stockholm, January 12, 1917

In June 1999, the Swedish Parliament enacted legislation giving Yiddish legal status[[104]](#cite_note-108) as one of the country's [official minority languages](/wiki/Official_minority_languages_of_Sweden "Official minority languages of Sweden") (entering into effect in April 2000).

Additional legislation was enacted in June 2006 establishing a new governmental agency, the Swedish National Language Council, whose goal is to "collect, preserve, scientifically research, and spread material about the national minority languages." These languages include Yiddish.

The Swedish government has published documents in Yiddish detailing the national action plan for human rights.[[105]](#cite_note-109) An earlier one provides general information about national minority language policies.[[106]](#cite_note-110) On September 6, 2007, it became possible to register Internet domains with Yiddish names in the national top-level domain [.se](/wiki/.se ".se").[[107]](#cite_note-111)

The first Jews were permitted to reside in Sweden during the late 18th century. The Jewish population in Sweden is estimated at 20,000. According to various reports and surveys, between 2,000 and 6,000 Swedish Jews have at least some knowledge of Yiddish. In 2009, the number of native speakers was estimated by linguist Mikael Parkvall to be 750–1,500. He says that most native speakers of Yiddish in Sweden today are adults, many of them elderly.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

After the war Yiddish theater enjoyed great popularity in Sweden and all the great stars performed there. Since the recognition of Yiddish as an official minority language, Swedish schoolchildren have the right to study Yiddish at school as a mother tongue, and there are public radio broadcasts and television shows in Yiddish.[[109]](#cite_note-113)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Free_Classes_in_English.jpg/250px-Free_Classes_in_English.jpg)](/wiki/File:Free_Classes_in_English.jpg)

Poster by the [City of New York](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City") advertising free English classes for Yiddish émigrés and proletarian speakers, 1930s:  
"Learn to speak, read and write the language of your children."

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Women_voter_outreach_1935_English_Yiddish.jpg/250px-Women_voter_outreach_1935_English_Yiddish.jpg)](/wiki/File:Women_voter_outreach_1935_English_Yiddish.jpg)

Women surrounded by posters in English and Yiddish supporting [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") candidates [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), [Herbert H. Lehman](/wiki/Herbert_H._Lehman "Herbert H. Lehman"), and the [American Labor Party](/wiki/American_Labor_Party "American Labor Party") teach other women how to vote, 1936.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Yiddish_language_distribution_in_the_United_States.svg/250px-Yiddish_language_distribution_in_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Yiddish_language_distribution_in_the_United_States.svg)
