In the United States, at first most Jews were of [Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardi_Jews "Sephardi Jews") origin, and hence did not speak Yiddish. It was not until the mid-to-late 19th century, as first German Jews, then Central and Eastern European Jews, arrived in the nation, that Yiddish became dominant within the immigrant community. This helped to bond Jews from many countries. פֿאָרװערטס (*Forverts* – *[The Forward](/wiki/The_Forward "The Forward")*) was one of seven Yiddish daily newspapers in New York City, and other Yiddish newspapers served as a forum for Jews of all European backgrounds. In 1915, the circulation of the daily Yiddish newspapers was half a million in New York City alone, and 600,000 nationally. In addition, thousands more subscribed to the numerous weekly papers and the many magazines.[[110]](#cite_note-114)

The typical circulation in the 21st century is a few thousand. The *Forward* still appears weekly and is also available in an online edition.[[111]](#cite_note-115) It remains in wide distribution, together with דער אַלגעמיינער זשורנאַל (*der algemeyner zhurnal* – *[Algemeyner Journal](/wiki/Algemeiner_Journal "Algemeiner Journal")*; *algemeyner* = general), a [Chabad](/wiki/Chabad "Chabad") newspaper which is also published weekly and appears online.[[112]](#cite_note-116) The widest-circulation Yiddish newspapers are probably the weekly issues *[Der Yid](/wiki/Der_Yid "Der Yid")* (דער איד "The Jew"), *[Der Blatt](/wiki/Der_Blatt "Der Blatt")* (דער בלאַט; *blat* 'paper') and *[Di Tzeitung](/wiki/Di_Tzeitung "Di Tzeitung")* (די צײטונג 'the newspaper'). Several additional newspapers and magazines are in regular production, such as the weekly אידישער טריביון *Yiddish Tribune* and the monthly publications דער שטערן (*Der Shtern* *The Star*) and דער בליק (*Der Blik* *The View*). (The romanized titles cited in this paragraph are in the form given on the masthead of each publication and may be at some variance both with the literal Yiddish title and the [transliteration rules](/wiki/Yiddish_orthography#Transliteration "Yiddish orthography") otherwise applied in this article.) Thriving Yiddish theater, especially in the New York City [Yiddish Theatre District](/wiki/Yiddish_Theatre_District "Yiddish Theatre District"), kept the language vital. Interest in [klezmer](/wiki/Klezmer "Klezmer") music provided another bonding mechanism.

Most of the Jewish immigrants to the New York metropolitan area during the years of [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") considered Yiddish their native language; however, native Yiddish speakers tended not to pass the language on to their children, who assimilated and spoke English. For example, [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") states in his autobiography *[In Memory Yet Green](/wiki/In_Memory_Yet_Green "In Memory Yet Green")* that Yiddish was his first and sole spoken language, and remained so for about two years after he emigrated to the United States as a small child. By contrast, Asimov's younger siblings, born in the United States, never developed any degree of fluency in Yiddish.

Many "Yiddishisms", like "Italianisms" and "Spanishisms", entered [New York City English](/wiki/New_York_City_English "New York City English"), often used by Jews and non-Jews alike, unaware of the linguistic origin of the phrases. [Yiddish words used in English](/wiki/Yiddish_words_used_in_English "Yiddish words used in English") were documented extensively by [Leo Rosten](/wiki/Leo_Rosten "Leo Rosten") in *[The Joys of Yiddish](/wiki/The_Joys_of_Yiddish "The Joys of Yiddish")*;[[113]](#cite_note-117) see also the [list of English words of Yiddish origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Yiddish_origin "List of English words of Yiddish origin").

In 1975, the film *[Hester Street](/wiki/Hester_Street_(film) "Hester Street (film)")*, much of which is in Yiddish, was released. It was later chosen to be on the Library of Congress [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") for being considered a "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant" film.[[114]](#cite_note-118)

In 1976, the Canadian-born American author [Saul Bellow](/wiki/Saul_Bellow "Saul Bellow") received the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"). He was fluent in Yiddish, and translated several Yiddish poems and stories into English, including [Isaac Bashevis Singer](/wiki/Isaac_Bashevis_Singer "Isaac Bashevis Singer")'s "Gimpel the Fool". In 1978, Singer, a writer in the Yiddish language, who was born in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and lived in the United States, received the Nobel Prize in Literature.

Legal scholars [Eugene Volokh](/wiki/Eugene_Volokh "Eugene Volokh") and [Alex Kozinski](/wiki/Alex_Kozinski "Alex Kozinski") argue that Yiddish is "supplanting Latin as the spice in American legal argot".[[115]](#cite_note-119)[[116]](#cite_note-120)

#### Present U.S. speaker population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Present U.S. speaker population")]

In the [2000 United States census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), 178,945 people in the United States reported speaking Yiddish at home. Of these speakers, 113,515 lived in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (63.43% of American Yiddish speakers); 18,220 in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") (10.18%); 9,145 in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") (5.11%); and 8,950 in [California](/wiki/California "California") (5.00%). The remaining states with speaker populations larger than 1,000 are [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") (5,445), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") (1,925), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") (1,945), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") (2,380), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") (2,125), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") (3,510), [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") (1,710), and [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") (1,055). The population is largely elderly: 72,885 of the speakers were older than 65, 66,815 were between 18 and 64, and only 39,245 were age 17 or lower.[[117]](#cite_note-121)

In the six years since the 2000 census, the 2006 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") reflected an estimated 15 percent decline of people speaking Yiddish at home in the U.S. to 152,515.[[118]](#cite_note-122) In 2011, the number of persons in the United States above the age of five speaking Yiddish at home was 160,968.[[119]](#cite_note-123) 88% of them were living in four [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") – New York City and another metropolitan area [just north of it](/wiki/Poughkeepsie%E2%80%93Newburgh%E2%80%93Middletown_metropolitan_area "Poughkeepsie–Newburgh–Middletown metropolitan area"), Miami, and Los Angeles.[[120]](#cite_note-124)

There are a few predominantly [Hasidic](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism") communities in the United States in which Yiddish remains the majority language including concentrations in the [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), [Borough Park](/wiki/Borough_Park,_Brooklyn "Borough Park, Brooklyn"), and [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn") neighborhoods of Brooklyn. In [Kiryas Joel](/wiki/Kiryas_Joel,_New_York "Kiryas Joel, New York") in [Orange County, New York](/wiki/Orange_County,_New_York "Orange County, New York"), in the 2000 census, nearly 90% of residents of Kiryas Joel reported speaking Yiddish at home.[[121]](#cite_note-125)[[122]](#cite_note-126)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

There are over 30,000 Yiddish speakers in the United Kingdom, and several thousand children now have Yiddish as a first language. The largest group of Yiddish speakers in Britain reside in the [Stamford Hill](/wiki/Stamford_Hill "Stamford Hill") district of North London, but there are sizable communities in northwest London, [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), Manchester and [Gateshead](/wiki/Gateshead "Gateshead").[[123]](#cite_note-127) The Yiddish readership in the UK is mainly reliant upon imported material from the United States and Israel for newspapers, magazines and other periodicals. However, the London-based weekly *[Jewish Tribune](/wiki/Jewish_Tribune_(UK) "Jewish Tribune (UK)")* has a small section in Yiddish called אידישע טריבונע *Yidishe Tribune*. From the 1910s to the 1950s, London had a daily Yiddish newspaper called די צײַט (*Di Tsayt*, Yiddish pronunciation: [[dɪ tsaɪt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Yiddish "Help:IPA/Yiddish"); in English, *The Time*), founded, and edited from offices in [Whitechapel Road](/wiki/Whitechapel_Road "Whitechapel Road"), by Romanian-born Morris Myer, who was succeeded on his death in 1943 by his son Harry. There were also from time to time Yiddish newspapers in Manchester, [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") and Leeds. The bilingual Yiddish and English café [Pink Peacock](/wiki/Pink_Peacock "Pink Peacock") opened in Glasgow in 2021 but closed down in 2023.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Canada")]

[Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") had, and to some extent still has, one of the most thriving Yiddish communities in North America. Yiddish was Montreal's third language (after French and English) for the entire first half of the twentieth century. *Der [Keneder Adler](/wiki/Keneder_Adler "Keneder Adler")* (*The Canadian Eagle*, founded by [Hirsch Wolofsky](/wiki/Hirsch_Wolofsky "Hirsch Wolofsky")), Montreal's daily Yiddish newspaper, appeared from 1907 to 1988.[[124]](#cite_note-128) The [Monument-National](/wiki/Monument-National "Monument-National") was the center of Yiddish theater from 1896 until the construction of the Saidye Bronfman Centre for the Arts (now the [Segal Centre for Performing Arts](/wiki/Segal_Centre_for_Performing_Arts "Segal Centre for Performing Arts")), inaugurated on September 24, 1967, where the established resident theater, the [Dora Wasserman Yiddish Theatre](/wiki/Dora_Wasserman_Yiddish_Theatre "Dora Wasserman Yiddish Theatre"), remains the only permanent Yiddish theatre in North America. The theatre group also tours Canada, US, Israel, and Europe.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

Even though Yiddish has receded, it is the immediate ancestral language of Montrealers like [Mordecai Richler](/wiki/Mordecai_Richler "Mordecai Richler") and [Leonard Cohen](/wiki/Leonard_Cohen "Leonard Cohen"), as well as former interim city mayor [Michael Applebaum](/wiki/Michael_Applebaum "Michael Applebaum"). Besides Yiddish-speaking activists, it remains today the native everyday language of 15,000 Montreal Hasidim.

### Religious communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Religious communities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Brooklyn_Posters_1.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Posters_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Posters_1.jpg)

A typical poster-hung wall in a Jewish section of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), New York

Major exceptions to the decline of spoken Yiddish are found in [Haredi](/wiki/Haredi_Judaism "Haredi Judaism") communities all over the world. In some of the more closely knit such communities, Yiddish is spoken as a home and schooling language, especially in Hasidic, [Litvish](/wiki/Lithuanian_Jews "Lithuanian Jews"), or Yeshivish communities, such as [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn")'s [Borough Park](/wiki/Borough_Park,_Brooklyn "Borough Park, Brooklyn"), [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn"), and [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), and in the communities of [Monsey](/wiki/Monsey,_New_York "Monsey, New York"), [Kiryas Joel](/wiki/Kiryas_Joel,_New_York "Kiryas Joel, New York"), and [New Square](/wiki/New_Square,_New_York "New Square, New York") in New York (over 88% of the population of Kiryas Joel is reported to speak Yiddish at home.[[126]](#cite_note-130)) Also in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), Yiddish is widely spoken mostly in [Lakewood Township](/wiki/Lakewood_Township,_New_Jersey "Lakewood Township, New Jersey"), but also in smaller towns with [yeshivas](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva"), such as [Passaic](/wiki/Passaic,_New_Jersey "Passaic, New Jersey"), [Teaneck](/wiki/Teaneck,_New_Jersey "Teaneck, New Jersey"), and elsewhere. Yiddish is also widely spoken in the Jewish community in [Antwerp](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Antwerp "History of the Jews in Antwerp"), and in Haredi communities such as the ones in [London](/wiki/London "London"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"). Yiddish is also spoken in many Haredi communities throughout Israel. Among most Ashkenazi Haredim, Hebrew is generally reserved for prayer, while Yiddish is used for religious studies, as well as a home and business language. In Israel, however, Haredim commonly speak [modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew"), with the notable exception of many Hasidic communities. However, many Haredim who use Modern Hebrew also understand Yiddish. There are some who send their children to schools in which the primary language of instruction is Yiddish. Members of anti-Zionist Haredi groups such as the [Satmar Hasidim](/wiki/Satmar_(Hasidic_dynasty) "Satmar (Hasidic dynasty)"), who view the commonplace use of Hebrew as a form of Zionism, use Yiddish almost exclusively.

Hundreds of thousands of young children around the globe have been, and are still, taught to translate the texts of the [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") into Yiddish. This process is called טײַטשן (*taytshn*) – 'translating'. Many Ashkenazi yeshivas' highest level lectures in Talmud and [Halakha](/wiki/Halakha "Halakha") are delivered in Yiddish by the [rosh yeshivas](/wiki/Rosh_yeshiva "Rosh yeshiva") as well as ethical talks of the [Musar movement](/wiki/Musar_movement "Musar movement"). Hasidic [rebbes](/wiki/Rebbe "Rebbe") generally use only Yiddish to converse with their followers and to deliver their various Torah talks, classes, and lectures. The linguistic style and vocabulary of Yiddish have influenced the manner in which many [Orthodox Jews](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") who attend yeshivas speak English. This usage is distinctive enough that it has been dubbed "[Yeshivish](/wiki/Yeshivish "Yeshivish")".

While Hebrew remains the exclusive language of [Jewish prayer](/wiki/Jewish_prayer "Jewish prayer"), the Hasidim have mixed some Yiddish into their Hebrew, and are also responsible for a significant secondary religious literature written in Yiddish. For example, the tales about the [Baal Shem Tov](/wiki/Baal_Shem_Tov "Baal Shem Tov") were written largely in Yiddish. The Torah Talks of the late Chabad leaders are published in their original form, Yiddish. In addition, some prayers, such as "[God of Abraham](/wiki/God_of_Abraham_(prayer) "God of Abraham (prayer)")", were composed and are recited in Yiddish.

### Modern Yiddish education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Modern Yiddish education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Yiddishsign.JPG/250px-Yiddishsign.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yiddishsign.JPG)

A road sign in Yiddish (except for the word "sidewalk") at an official construction site in the [Monsey](/wiki/Monsey,_New_York "Monsey, New York") hamlet, a community with thousands of Yiddish speakers, in [Ramapo](/wiki/Ramapo,_New_York "Ramapo, New York"), New York

There has been a resurgence in Yiddish learning in recent times among many from around the world with Jewish ancestry. The language which had lost many of its native speakers during the Holocaust has been making something of a comeback.[[127]](#cite_note-131) In Poland, which traditionally had Yiddish speaking communities, a museum has begun to revive Yiddish education and culture.[[128]](#cite_note-132) Located in Kraków, the [Galicia Jewish Museum](/wiki/Galicia_Jewish_Museum "Galicia Jewish Museum") offers classes in Yiddish Language Instruction and workshops on Yiddish songs. The museum has taken steps to revive the culture through concerts and events held on site.[[129]](#cite_note-133)
There are various universities worldwide which now offer Yiddish programs based on the [YIVO](/wiki/YIVO "YIVO") Yiddish standard. Many of these programs are held during the summer and are attended by Yiddish enthusiasts from around the world. One such school located within [Vilnius University](/wiki/Vilnius_University "Vilnius University") (Vilnius Yiddish Institute) was the first Yiddish center of higher learning to be established in post-Holocaust Eastern Europe. Vilnius Yiddish Institute is an integral part of the four-century-old Vilnius University. Published Yiddish scholar and researcher Dovid Katz is among the Faculty.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

Despite this growing popularity among many [American Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews"),[[131]](#cite_note-135) finding opportunities for practical use of Yiddish is becoming increasingly difficult, and thus many students have trouble learning to speak the language.[[132]](#cite_note-136) One solution has been the establishment of a farm in [Goshen, New York](/wiki/Goshen,_New_York "Goshen, New York"), for Yiddishists.[[133]](#cite_note-137)

Yiddish is the medium of instruction in many Hasidic חדרים *khadorim*, Jewish boys' schools, and some Hasidic girls' schools.

Some American Jewish day schools and high schools offer Yiddish education.

An organization called *Yiddishkayt (ייִדישקײַט)* promotes Yiddish-language education in schools.[[134]](#cite_note-138)

[Sholem Aleichem College](/wiki/Sholem_Aleichem_College "Sholem Aleichem College"), a secular Jewish primary school in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") teaches Yiddish as a second language to all its students. The school was founded in 1975 by the [Bund movement in Australia](/wiki/Jewish_Labour_Bund_(Australia) "Jewish Labour Bund (Australia)"), and still maintains daily Yiddish instruction today, and includes student theater and music in Yiddish.

### Internet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Internet")]

[Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate") includes Yiddish as one of its languages,[[135]](#cite_note-139)[[136]](#cite_note-140) as does [Wikipedia](/wiki/Yiddish_Wikipedia "Yiddish Wikipedia"). Hebrew-alphabet keyboards are available, and right-to-left writing is recognized. [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") accepts queries in Yiddish.

Over eleven thousand Yiddish texts,[[137]](#cite_note-141) estimated as between a sixth and a quarter of all the published works in Yiddish,[[138]](#cite_note-142) are now online, based on the work of the [Yiddish Book Center](/wiki/Yiddish_Book_Center "Yiddish Book Center"), volunteers, and the [Internet Archive](/wiki/Internet_Archive "Internet Archive").[[139]](#cite_note-143)

There are many websites on the Internet in Yiddish. In January 2013, *The Forward* announced the launch of the new daily version of its newspaper's website, which has been active since 1999 as an online weekly, supplied with radio and video programs, a literary section for fiction writers and a special blog written in local contemporary Hasidic dialects.[[140]](#cite_note-144)

Many Jewish [ethnolects](/wiki/Ethnolect "Ethnolect") influenced by Yiddish are available via online resources such as [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube").[[141]](#cite_note-:4-145)

Computer scientist [Raphael Finkel](/wiki/Raphael_Finkel "Raphael Finkel") maintains a hub of Yiddish-language resources, including a searchable [dictionary](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary")[[142]](#cite_note-146) and [spell checker](/wiki/Spell_checker "Spell checker").[[143]](#cite_note-147)

In late 2016, [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola") Inc. released its smartphones with keyboard access for the Yiddish language in its foreign language option.

On April 5, 2021, [Duolingo](/wiki/Duolingo "Duolingo") added Yiddish to its courses.[[144]](#cite_note-148)

## Influence on other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Influence on other languages")]

In addition to [Modern Hebrew](/wiki/Modern_Hebrew "Modern Hebrew") and [New York English](/wiki/New_York_English "New York English"), especially as spoken by [yeshivah](/wiki/Yeshivah "Yeshivah") students (sometimes known as [Yeshivish](/wiki/Yeshivish "Yeshivish")), Yiddish has influenced [Cockney](/wiki/Cockney_English "Cockney English") in [England](/wiki/England "England"), the city dialect of [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") and to some degree the city dialects of [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") and [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"). [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") [argot](/wiki/Argot "Argot") has some words coming from Yiddish.[[145]](#cite_note-Nahon_2017_pp._139–155-149)

[Paul Wexler](/wiki/Paul_Wexler_(linguist) "Paul Wexler (linguist)") proposed that [Esperanto](/wiki/Esperanto "Esperanto") was not an arbitrary pastiche of major European languages but a Latinate [relexification](/wiki/Relexification "Relexification") of Yiddish, a native language of its [founder](/wiki/L._L._Zamenhof "L. L. Zamenhof").[[146]](#cite_note-Wexler-150) This model is generally unsupported by mainstream linguists.[[147]](#cite_note-Spolsky_157-151)

Yiddish had an influence on [Jewish Swedish](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_Swedish&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jewish Swedish (page does not exist)"), a dialect of [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") used by Jews with loanwords from Hebrew and Yiddish.[[148]](#cite_note-152) And Yiddish had an influence on Hungarian with extra influence on [Jewish Hungarian](/w/index.php?title=Judeo_Hungarian&action=edit&redlink=1 "Judeo Hungarian (page does not exist)"), a dialect of Hungarian spoken by [Hungarian Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Hungary "History of the Jews in Hungary").[[149]](#cite_note-153) Yiddish had influence on other [European Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Europe "History of the Jews in Europe") ethnolects like [Jewish Russian](/w/index.php?title=Judeo_Russian&action=edit&redlink=1 "Judeo Russian (page does not exist)") and [Jewish French](/wiki/Varieties_of_French "Varieties of French").[[150]](#cite_note-Jewish_Russian-154) These ethnolects are shown in various pieces of media across various mediums both digital and physical.[[150]](#cite_note-Jewish_Russian-154)[[141]](#cite_note-:4-145)

## Language examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yiddish&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Language examples")]

Example video of native Yiddish speaker talking in the Yiddish language (with English-translated subtitles)

The following is a short example of the Yiddish language written in both the Hebrew and Latin scripts with English and [standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") for comparison.

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights")

| Language | Text |
| --- | --- |
| **English**[[151]](#cite_note-155) | All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood. |
| **Yiddish**[[152]](#cite_note-ohchr_yiddish-156) | יעדער מענטש װערט געבױרן פֿרײַ און גלײַך אין כּבֿוד און רעכט. יעדער װערט באַשאָנקן מיט פֿאַרשטאַנד און געװיסן; יעדער זאָל זיך פֿירן מיט אַ צװײטן אין אַ געמיט פֿון ברודערשאַפֿט. |
| **Yiddish** **(transliteration)** | *yeder mentsh vert geboyrn fray un glaykh in koved un rekht. yeder vert bashonkn mit farshtand un gevisn; yeder zol zikh firn mit a tsveytn in a gemit fun brudershaft.* |
| **German with wording and word order as close to the Yiddish version as possible** | *Jeder Mensch wird geboren, frei und gleich in Ehre und Recht. Jeder wird beschenkt, mit Verstand und Gewissen; Jeder soll sich führen, miteinander im Gemüt von Bruderschaft.* |
| **Official German version**[[153]](#cite_note-157) | *Alle Menschen sind frei und gleich an Würde und Rechten geboren. Sie sind mit Vernunft und Gewissen begabt und sollen einander im Geist der Brüderlichkeit begegnen.* |
| Hebrew[[154]](#cite_note-158) | כל בני האדם נולדו בני חורין ושווים בערכם ובזכויותיהם. כולם חוננו בתבונה ובמצפון, לפיכך חובה עליהם לנהוג איש ברעהו ברוח של אחווה. |
| Hebrew (transliteration) | *kol benei ha'adam noldu benei khorin veshavim be'erkam uvizkhuyoteihem. Kulam khonenu bitevuna uvematspun, lefikhakh chova 'aleihem linhog ish bere'ehu beruakh shel akhava.* |
